narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail it. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually stronger than the typical applicant's 3.21—your LSAT score of 36, your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 5 and 4 respectively, your upper-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. For a Black male student like yourself, with these solid credentials on paper, the model still tipped toward rejection due to a few key factors that outweighed the positives. The three most influential elements in this decision were your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and Year 3 decile ranking. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive pull, nudging the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it stands out favorably compared to most applicants. Similarly, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pushing the outlook toward success by aligning closely with high-performing students. However, your Year 3 decile ranking of 4—which is below the average of 5.53—had the most decisive counter-effect, significantly increasing the model's concern about bar exam failure by signaling a dip in later academic performance relative to peers. While your full-time status is the norm for over 90% of students and didn't sway things much, these top factors combined to create enough doubt about first-try bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment aimed at ensuring incoming classes succeed. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above the 36.70 average or strengthening your recent grades to improve that Year 3 decile—perhaps through additional coursework or targeted prep. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing these areas, and admissions teams often review appeals with new evidence of growth. You're clearly capable, given your GPA and LSAT strengths, so focusing here could turn this around.
